questions.question: A spring has an original length of $16.0\text{ cm}$. When a load of $2.0\text{ N}$ is attached, the length becomes $20.0\text{ cm}$. Calculate the extension.
questions.explanation:
Extension $x = l - l_0 = 20.0\text{ cm} - 16.0\text{ cm} = 4.0\text{ cm}$. 

**Common mistake:** Adding the lengths together instead of subtracting the original length from the new length.

questions.question: A force of $10\text{ N}$ stretches a spring by $50\text{ mm}$. Calculate the spring constant $k$.
questions.explanation:
Using $k = F / x$: $k = 10\text{ N} / 50\text{ mm} = 0.2\text{ N/mm}$. 

**Common mistake:** Multiplying force and extension ($500$) instead of dividing force by extension.

questions.question: If spring A has a steeper gradient on a load–extension graph than spring B, which statement is correct?
options: Spring A has a larger spring constant than spring B
Spring A is more easily stretched than spring B
Spring B has a larger spring constant than spring A
Spring A and spring B have the same stiffness
questions.explanation:
A steeper gradient on a Load ($F$) vs Extension ($x$) graph means a higher ratio of $F/x$, which is a larger spring constant. 

**Common mistake:** Thinking a steeper line means it is 'easier' to stretch; it actually means it is 'stiffer' and harder to stretch.

questions.question: A bag of sugar has a mass of $1\text{ kg}$. Calculate its weight on Earth to determine the force it would exert on a spring. (Take $g = 10\text{ N/kg}$)
questions.explanation:
$W = mg = 1\text{ kg} \times 10\text{ N/kg} = 10\text{ N}$. 

**Common mistake:** Confusing mass and weight and thinking the force is $1\text{ N}$.

questions.question: A spring with $k = 0.2\text{ N/mm}$ is stretched by a watermelon that has a weight of $15\text{ N}$. What is the extension produced?
questions.explanation:
Rearranging $k = F/x$ gives $x = F / k = 15\text{ N} / 0.2\text{ N/mm} = 75\text{ mm}$. 

**Common mistake:** Multiplying the spring constant by the force ($3.0$).
